caption: Figure 1: LT-MIA transfers from transformers to non-transformer architectures, exceeding transformer performance on every text dataset. Blue bars show held-out transformer results (mean over 7 models), while the remaining bars show zero-shot transfer to Mamba-2.8B (state-space), RWKV-4-3B (linear attention), and RecurrentGemma-2B (gated recurrence). The classifier was trained exclusively on transformers and textual data.
description2: The figure plots a line chart comparing the performance of transformer-based models with non-transformer architectures, showing held-out results for transformers and zero-shot transfer to other models.
description3: Two-panel bar chart compares the performance of transformer, Mamba, RWKV, and RecurrentGemma models across four datasets, displaying AUC values on the y-axis.
questions: What main objective or task does the figure describe?
How is the overall pipeline or architecture organized in the figure?

caption: Figure 2: LT-MIA pipeline. Given a text sample and black-box access to a fine-tuned target model and its pre-trained reference, we extract 154-dimensional feature vectors at each token position capturing how the two models’ predictions diverge. A lightweight transformer encoder processes this feature sequence to predict membership.
description2: The LT-MIA pipeline diagram illustrates the extraction of 154-dimensional feature vectors from text samples, tracking divergence between fine-tuned and pre-trained target models.
description3: Two-panel diagram outlines a pipeline, beginning with text input, progressing through models and feature extraction, and concluding with a classifier output.
questions: How does this pipeline use model predictions to determine membership?
What is the general flow of information through this membership prediction process?

caption: Figure 3: Feature importance measured as AUC drop when each feature group is ablated. Comparison features (relating target to reference model outputs) dominate across all four architectural families. The consistent hierarchy (Comparison > Target-only > Reference-only) confirms the membership signal is relational: what matters is how fine-tuning changed the model, not the model’s behavior in isolation.
description2: The figure plots feature importance measured as AUC drop when each feature group is ablated, with comparison features dominating across four architectural families.
description3: Two-panel bar chart displays AUC drop values for comparison, target-only, and reference-only feature groups across Transformer, Mamba, RWKV, and RecurrentGemma architectures.
questions: How do architectural families compare regarding the importance of feature groups?
What general relationship is suggested by the consistent ranking of feature groups?

caption: Figure 4: Effect of training diversity on generalization, with total samples fixed at 18,000. “Train AUC” is evaluated on held-out samples from training model-dataset combinations (in-distribution), while “Eval AUC” is evaluated on entirely different model-dataset combinations (out-of-distribution). All metrics use samples never seen during classifier training. Training on one combination yields near-perfect in-distribution AUC (0.998) but poor transfer (0.796). With 30 combinations, the gap shrinks from 20.2 points to 0.2 points.
description2: The figure plots the effect of training diversity on generalization, with total samples fixed at 18,000, evaluating model-dataset combinations' performance in- and out-of-distribution.
description3: Two-panel bar chart displays “Train AUC” and “Eval AUC” values across one, ten, and thirty training combinations, with total samples fixed at 18,000.
questions: How does the number of training combinations affect generalization performance?
What is the relationship between in-distribution and out-of-distribution evaluation metrics?
